coredata.dc:title: Feasibility of detection valence speciation of Cr(III) and Cr(VI) in environmental samples by spectrofluorimetric method with fluorescent carbon quantum dots
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Feng, Shouai
Gao, Zhe
Liu, Hong
Huang, Jiangfeng
Li, Xiaolan
Yang, Yaling
coredata.dc:description: A simple and fast spectrofluorimetric method coupled with fluorescent carbon quantum dots have been developed for the detection and speciation chromium(VI) and chromium(III). The high fluorescence quantum yield (QY: 20.5%) carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were synthesized from natural kelp by hydrothermal method. The fluorescence of CQDs was effectively quenched by Cr(VI) due to inner filter effect (IFE) and the strong interactions between the functional groups on the CQDs surface and Cr(VI). Cr(III) species after oxidation of this species to Cr(VI) using KMnO4 as the oxidant were also detected by the proposed method. Under optimal conditions, the Cr(VI) could be detected with the range from 0.01 to 50 μM and a limit of detection 0.52 μM. And the synthesized CQDs were utilized as fluorescent probe to determinate Cr(III) and Cr(VI) in environmental water samples with satisfactory recoveries in the range of 94%–107%.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fluorescent
Carbon quantum dots
Cr(III)
Cr(VI)
Detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of CQDs From the Natural Kelp
Selectivity and Sensitivity of the Probe
Detection of Cr(III) and Cr(VI) in Environmental Samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) UV absorption and FL excitation, emission spectra of CQDs. (b) FL emission spectra of CQDs at different excitation wavelengths ranging from 310 nm to 410 nm. (c) TEM image of CQDs. (d) FTIR spectrum of as-prepared CQDs. (e) XRD pattern of CQDs. (f) XPS spectra of CQDs. (g) C1s high-resolution XPS spectra. (h) N1s high-resolution XPS spectra. (i) O1s high-resolution XPS spectra.
Fluorescence response of CQDs in the absence and presence of different metalions.
(a) Fluorescence spectra of CQDs with the addition of different concentrations of Cr(VI). (b) Calibration curve for Cr(VI).
(a) UV–vis absorption spectrum of Cr(VI) and excitation and emission spectrum of CQDs. (b) Fluorescence decay of CQDs in the absence and presence of Cr(VI).
Schematic illustration of the preparation of the CQDs and for Cr(VI) and total Cr detection.
The analysis results of certified reference materials (n = 3). a
Determination results of Cr(III) and Cr(VI) in water samples (n = 3).
Comparison of methods for Cr(VI) determination.

head.ce:title: Feasibility of detection valence speciation of Cr(III) and Cr(VI) in environmental samples by spectrofluorimetric method with fluorescent carbon quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Technology Centre of China Tobacco Guangxi Industrial Co., LTD, Nanning 530001, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Technology Centre of China Tobacco Guangxi Industrial Co., LTD

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Faculty of Life Science and Technology, Kunming University of Science and Technology, Kunming 650500, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Faculty of Life Science and Technology
Kunming University of Science and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A simple and fast spectrofluorimetric method coupled with fluorescent carbon quantum dots have been developed for the detection and speciation chromium(VI) and chromium(III). The high fluorescence quantum yield (QY: 20.5%) carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were synthesized from natural kelp by hydrothermal method. The fluorescence of CQDs was effectively quenched by Cr(VI) due to inner filter effect (IFE) and the strong interactions between the functional groups on the CQDs surface and Cr(VI). Cr(III) species after oxidation of this species to Cr(VI) using KMnO4 as the oxidant were also detected by the proposed method. Under optimal conditions, the Cr(VI) could be detected with the range from 0.01 to 50 μM and a limit of detection 0.52 μM. And the synthesized CQDs were utilized as fluorescent probe to determinate Cr(III) and Cr(VI) in environmental water samples with satisfactory recoveries in the range of 94%–107%.

ce:section.ce:para: Chromium is considered as a toxic element and depending on chromium species emerge different toxicities. Cr(III) and Cr(VI) can be found in the environment, and Cr(VI) is highly toxic even in low concentrations due to its higher oxidation potential and high carcinogenicity [1–3]. Thus, a permissible below 0.96 μM concentration of Cr(VI) in drink water is sternly prescribed to by many nations [4]. Owing to the different toxicities of Cr(III) and Cr(VI) species, the speciation analysis of Cr for a simple and precise procedure in water samples is very important [5,6]. Several available techniques for the speciation analysis of Cr in environmental samples have been explored, such as voltammetry [7], atomic absorption spectrometry (AAS) [8,9], ultraviolet visible absorption spectrometry (UV–vis) [10], X-ray fluorescence spectrometry [11], inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS) [12], etc. Although these methods are sensitive, they are not suitable for industrial applications due to their complicated sample preparation processes, expensive equipment, and lack of timely and rapid detection. Compared with the methods mentioned above, the fluorescence probe method has the advantages of simple sample pretreatment, fast response, a wide linear dynamic range, lower interference, and high sensitivity, which can satisfy the requirements of industrial application.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have attracted tremendous attention due to excellent properties, including high chemical stability [13,14], excellent water solubility [15,16], facile synthesis route [17], bright fluorescence [18], high resistance to photo bleaching, and low toxicity [19–22]. Especially, because the fluorescent CQDs usually have high quantum yield (QY), a series of fluorescent methods basing on CQDs in metal ions including Cr(VI) detection have been developed in recent years [23–25]. CQDs have been synthesized by means of hydrothermal carbonization methods or microwave-assisted pyrolysis, using citric acid and ethylene diamine and other chemical material and using “green” raw as carbon sources materials rarely reported [26,27]. X. Zhu et al. reported the synthesis of carbon dots using kelp juice for detection of pH and Cu2+ ions in real samples [28]. The fluorescence quenching mechanism induced by metal ions is vague at present. Most researchers attribute the quenching mechanism to the electron, charge or energy transfer due to the interaction between CDs and metal ions [29]. The inner filter effect (IFE) is considered another reason for the fluorescence quenching [30]. IFE, which arises from the absorption of the excitation or emission light by absorbers in the detection system [31], has been proven to enhance the sensitivity compared to other conventional fluorescent quenching mechanisms [32–34]. Moreover, the detection system based on IFE is simple and flexible because it does not need covalent interaction between receptor and fluorophore.

In this work, a novel fluorescence analysis method for detecting Cr(III) and Cr(VI) was proposed in environmental water samples. The high quantum yield fluorescent carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were synthesized from natural kelp via a one-step hydrothermal method. The CQDs were characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM), X-ray diffraction (XRD), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), ultraviolet-visible (UV–vis) spectroscopy and fluorescence spectroscopy. We proposed an effective IFE-based fluorescence sensor for the detection of Cr(VI) with high selectivity. Cr(III) species after oxidation of this species to Cr(VI) using KMnO4 as the oxidant were also detected by the proposed method. This approach was successfully applied for detection of Cr(III) and Cr(VI) in environmental samples (Scheme 1 ).

The natural kelp was purchased from the local supermarket of Kunming, China. All the reagents used were of analytic grade. The stock standard solutions of the Cr(III) and Cr(VI) ions (1 mM) were prepared, separately, by dissolving appropriate amounts of CrCl3 and K2CrO4, respectively, in deionized water. Sodium chloride was obtained from Fisher, Belgium, and 65% nitric acid from Merck, Germany. The different metal ions Cu2+(CuCl2), Zn2+(CuCl2), Ni2+(NiCl2), Hg2+(Hg(NO3)2), Fe2+(FeCl2), Fe3+(FeCl3), Co2+(CoCl2), Ca2+(CaCl2) and Al3+(CuCl2), Pb2+(Pb(NO3)2), and Cd2+(Cd(Ac)2) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich and used as received. Deionized (DI) water was used in all experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of CQDs From the Natural Kelp
ce:section.ce:para: The CQDs were synthesized by one-step hydrothermal method using natural kelp (one of the algae plantscontains-crude proteins: 8.2 g, fats: 0.1 g, sugar: 57 g, crude fiber: 9.8 g, inorganic salt: 12.9 g, calcium: 2.25 g, iron: 0.15 g, thiamine: 0.69 mg, riboflavin: 0.36 mg, niacin: 16 mg) as a carbon source. Briefly, 10 g of dry and clean kelp was added into 100 mL of deionized water, followed by breaking fresh kelp into kelp juice, and was transferred into Teflon-lined autoclave, which was heated for 6 h at 180 °C. Afterward, the autoclave was cooled to room temperature. The product was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 10 min and the resultant supernatant was filtered through the 0.22 μm membrane. The result and filtrate containing CQDs was further dialyzed with a dialysis membrane (1000 Da) for purification and stored at 4 °C in refrigerator before used in further experiments.

The morphology and microstructure of the CQDs were examined by TEM on a TecnaiG2 TF30 instrument with an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. The samples for TEM were prepared by dropping an aqueous solution onto carbon-coated copper grid then dried at room temperature for 12 h. The fluorescence spectra and the UV–vis absorption spectra were recorded by G9800A Cary Eclipse (Agilent Technologies, USA) fluorescence spectrophotometer and UV-2550 (Shimadzu, Japan) spectrophotometer, respectively. FT-IR spectrum was performed on a TENSOR27 FT-IR spectrometer (Bruker, Germany). X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) data were recorded on D/Max 2200 with Cu Kα radiation (γ = 1.5405 A) ranging from 2θ = 5° to 90° (Rigaku, America).

In atypical the detection of Cr(VI), 100 μL of CQDs and 0.4 mL of Cr(VI) (100 μM) solution were diluted by Tris-HCl buffer solution (10 mM, pH 5.0) to 2.0 mL. The mixture was vortexed for 30s and then incubation for 10 min, and the fluorescence emission spectra were measured at an excitation wavelength of 360 nm and at emission wavelength of 446 nm.

For the detection of total Cr, 0.20 mL of KMnO4 (0.05 mol L−1) and 1 mL of H2SO4 (0.1 mol L−1) solution were added into 10 mL the sample solution including Cr(VI) and Cr(III). The solution was heated for 30 min at 50 °C for completely oxidation. Then, the sodium nitrite (0.1 g) as the reducing agent was added into solution with the unreacted KMnO4. After the oxidation, total chromium concentration was measured according to the detection of Cr(VI).
For the detection of Cr(III), the concentration of Cr(III) in samples solution was subtracted Cr(VI) from the total chromium.

Fig. 1a shows the UV–vis absorption spectra and fluorescence emission and excitation spectra of CQDs. The CQDs show the UV–vis absorption peak at 288 nm, which confirms the presence of the n–π* transition and the contribution of surface moieties. The CQDs show the maximum emission intensity (QY = 20.5%, quinine sulphate as a reference) at 446 nm with an excitation wavelength of 360 nm. When the excitation wavelength is changed from 310 to 410 nm, the emission peak of CQDs was red-shifted with increasing emission peak intensity from 340 nm to 410 nm, and then the fluorescence intensity decreases remarkably (Fig. 1b). The result indicates that the fluorescence intensity of CQDs is strongly dependent on the corresponding excitation wavelength. Meanwhile, the natural kelp itself is non-emissive in the studied region, confirming the bright fluorescence is stemming from the synthesized CQDs.

Fig. 1c TEM shows the morphology and microstructure of the CQDs, revealing that these CQDs are regular spherical morphology and a relatively narrow size distribution between 2 and 4 nm. The lattice spacing is 0.23 nm, which is consistent with the typical distance between graphite (100) planes.

To identify the surface composition of the CQDs, FTIR spectrum was given in Fig. 1d. The characteristic absorption band at 3448 cm−1 is assigned to OH groups stretching vibration. The peaks at 2934 cm−1 and 1701 cm−1 can be associated with the CH and CO stretching vibration, respectively. The peak centered at 1380 cm−1was ascribed to the stretching vibrations of COOH on the surface of CQDs.

The XRD patterns of CQDs shows a resolvable broad peak centered at 25.98°, which is close to the diffraction peak of the graphite (002) crystal plane at 26.1°. Therefore, all of the above results demonstrate the existence of CQDs.

The XPS spectrum provided more convincing evidence for elemental contents and surface groups of the prepared CQDs. As shown in Fig. 1f, the full scan XPS spectra presented distinct peaks locating at 285.0, 399.7, and 532 eV, which are attributed to C1s, N1s, and O1s, respectively. This further corroborated that the prepared CQDs mainly contained carbon, nitrogen, and oxygen. The high-resolution spectrum of C1s (Fig. 1g) displayed four different types of surface components, corresponding to CC/CC at binding energies of 284.5 eV, CN at 285.6 eV and CO at 286.3 eV. For the high-resolution spectrum of N1 s (Fig. 3h), the peaks at 399.8 and 401.4 eV were assigned to the absorption of CNC and NH groups, respectively. In addition, the high-resolution spectrum of O1s (Fig. 1i) was fitted with two peaks at 530.1 eV and 532.3 eV, which are ascribed to CO and CO bonds. The surface components of CQDs determined by the XPS were in agreement with that of FT-IR results.

To evaluate the stability of CQDs, the effect of ionic strength on the fluorescence of CQDs was also examined in the presence of various concentrations of NaCl, and the fluorescence intensity of CQDs remained constant (Fig. S1). In addition, the fluorescence spectra of CQDs were measured after stored different days at 4 °C. Fig. S2 shows that the fluorescence intensity of CQDs maintains 95% of the original intensity even after stored one month, which indicates that the CQDs possess excellent stability.

To improve the sensitivity of Cr(VI) detection, the effect of pH value of Tris-HCl buffer, the amount of CQDs in analysis solution and incubation time between the CQDs and Cr(VI) on the detection were investigated, respectively. The quenching efficiency of Cr(VI) was calculated by F0/F, where F and F0 represent the fluorescence intensity of CQDs in the presence and absence of Cr(VI), respectively. Fig. S3a shows that the quenching efficiency reached the maximum at pH 5.0, CQDs of 100 μL and incubation time of 5 min, indicating the sensor exhibits the highest sensitivity under these conditions. Thus, the optimum reaction condition for Cr(VI) detection was different concentrations of Cr(VI) incubate with 100 μL CQDs in Tris-HCl buffer solution (10 mM, pH 5.0) for 5 min. Simultaneously, as shown in Fig. S3b, there were no significant changes in fluorescence intensity were observed after a reaction time of 10 min, in order to arrange experimental time reasonably, 10 min was chosen as the optimum reaction time.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity and Sensitivity of the Probe
ce:section.ce:para: The upon addition fluorescence quenching of CQDs by the addition of Cr(VI) allows application as a Cr(VI) sensor. The response of CQDs was examine of various metal ions such as (Al3+, Mg2+,Zn2+, Cu2+, Ba2+, Cd2+, Ag+,Pb2+,Ca2+, Mn2+, Hg2+and Fe3+, 100 μM),(NO3 −, SO4 2−, IO3 −, 100 μM) and (NO2 −, 500 μM) under the identical conditions of Cr(VI) (10 μM) at 360 nm excitation. The Fig. 2 showed that only Cr(VI) induced a prominent fluorescence intensity reduction, which makes the CQDs as highly selective fluorescent probes for Cr(VI).

Fig. 3a, b showed the fluorescence spectra of the CQDs after adding different concentrations of Cr(VI). The fluorescence intensity of the CQDs was quenched gradually while increasing the concentrations of Cr(VI) from 0.01 to 50 μM with a calibration function of F/F0 = −0.009 [Cr(VI)] (μM) + 0.982(R2 = 0.991)(F0 and F represent the fluorescence emission intensities of the CQDs in the absence and presence of Cr(VI), respectively (Fig. 3b). The detection limit of CQDs for Cr(VI) was as low as 0.52 μM. The results demonstrate that CQDs could serve as selective and sensitive fluorescent probe for the determination of Cr(VI) in real samples. Compared to the reported methods it demonstrated the developed approach provided a wide linear range and a low detection limit for the determination of Cr(VI) (Table 1 ).

IFE occurs when the absorption spectrum of quencher in the detection system overlapped with the excitation or emissionspectra of CQDs. As Fig. 4a shown, Cr(VI) exhibited absorption peaks at 272 and 371 nm, which precisely overlapped with the excitation wavelength at 360 nm of CQDs. Thus, the excitation wavelength for CQDs can be absorbed by Cr(VI), which resulted in the fluorescence of CQDs being effectively quenched by the absorber Cr(VI) and it is the so-called the inner filter effect (IFE). It suggests that the Cr(VI) induced fluorescence quenching of the CQDs may stem from the IFE. To further verify the quenching mechanism of CQDs toward Cr(VI), the lifetimes of CQDs without and with Cr(VI) were measured and the results were illustrated in Fig. 4b. The average lifetime values of CQDs and CQDs-Cr(VI) were 7.28 and 7.14 ns, respectively. The slightly changes of lifetime indicated that the fluorescence quenching of CQDs by Cr(VI) was ascribed to IFE.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Cr(III) and Cr(VI) in Environmental Samples

ce:section.ce:para: In summary, a facile synthesis of CQDs was developed by using natural kelp as a new class of carbon sources and hydrothermal method. The obtained CQDs exhibited high quantum yield (up to 20.5%), good photostability and excitation-independent emission behavior. Importantly, the CQDs can be used as a fluorescent probe for fast and effective detection of Cr(VI) and Cr(III) species. Moreover, the experimental results reveal that the fluorescence quenching mechanism arise from the inner filter effect (IFE) and the strong interactions between the functional groups on the CQDs surface and Cr(VI). We believe that the CQDs can be served as a facile fluorescent sensing platform for detection of Cr(VI) and Cr(III) species and have promising prospect in environment applications.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The work was strongly supported by the Analysis and Testing Foundation of Kunming University of Science and Technology (2018M20172118081).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2018.12.055.
